probe_designs.content: Compute AUROC, AUPRC, sensitivity, specificity, FPR, FNR, and calibration error per ethnicity group (the 6 one-hot categories). Quantify disparities using metrics such as Equal Opportunity Difference, Equalized Odds gap, and Demographic Parity Difference. Healthy: gaps in TPR/FPR < 0.05 across groups and AUROC variance small relative to bootstrap CI. Unhealthy: large between-group gaps (e.g., minority groups have FNR >> majority), or one ethnicity drives most errors. Use stratified bootstrapping to confirm gaps are not noise from small subgroup sizes.
possible_sources: Chen et al., 'Ethical Machine Learning in Healthcare' (Annual Review of Biomedical Data Science, 2021)
Hardt et al., 'Equality of Opportunity in Supervised Learning' (NeurIPS 2016)
Pfohl et al., 'An empirical characterization of fair machine learning for clinical risk prediction' (JBI 2021)
AIF360 toolkit (Bellamy et al., 2018)

probe_designs.content: Train and compare three model variants: (a) notes only (no ethnicity input), (b) ethnicity only, (c) notes + ethnicity. Then perform counterfactual flips of the ethnicity one-hot while holding TF-IDF fixed and measure prediction shift distribution. Healthy: ethnicity contributes minimally and counterfactual flips cause small, symmetric prediction changes. Unhealthy: model relies heavily on ethnicity feature, or counterfactuals systematically lower scores for specific groups, indicating direct disparate treatment.
possible_sources: Kusner et al., 'Counterfactual Fairness' (NeurIPS 2017)
Zhang et al., 'Hurtful Words: Quantifying Biases in Clinical Contextual Word Embeddings' (CHIL 2020)
Pfohl et al., 'Counterfactual Reasoning for Fair Clinical Risk Prediction' (MLHC 2019)

probe_designs.content: Plot reliability diagrams and compute Expected Calibration Error (ECE), Maximum Calibration Error (MCE), and Brier score overall and per ethnicity group. Optionally fit Platt scaling/isotonic regression per group. Healthy: ECE < 0.05 across all groups, reliability curves close to diagonal. Unhealthy: model is overconfident or underconfident for specific subgroups (e.g., predicted mortality of 0.3 corresponds to actual 0.6 in one ethnicity), which is a clinically dangerous miscalibration even if AUROC is balanced.
possible_sources: Guo et al., 'On Calibration of Modern Neural Networks' (ICML 2017)
Barda et al., 'Addressing bias in prediction models by improving subpopulation calibration' (JAMIA 2021)
Niculescu-Mizil & Caruana, 'Predicting good probabilities with supervised learning' (ICML 2005)

probe_designs.probe_name: Train/validation/test loss and AUROC trajectory inspection
probe_designs.content: Track training vs. validation loss, AUROC, and AUPRC across epochs. Inspect the gap between training and validation. Healthy: smooth convergence, small generalization gap, validation AUROC plateaus near training AUROC. Unhealthy: training loss falling while validation rises (overfitting), oscillation suggesting LR too high, or both flat near random suggesting underfitting given the 10k-dim sparse TF-IDF input where a small linear or shallow MLP head may be appropriate.
possible_sources: Goodfellow et al., 'Deep Learning' (MIT Press, ch. 7)
Caruana et al., 'Overfitting in Neural Nets: Backpropagation, Conjugate Gradient, and Early Stopping' (NeurIPS 2000)
Harutyunyan et al., 'Multitask learning and benchmarking with clinical time series data' (Sci Data 2019)

probe_designs.probe_name: TF-IDF feature distribution and class-imbalance audit
probe_designs.content: Inspect TF-IDF feature sparsity, vocabulary coverage per ethnicity group, mean note length, and label prevalence (mortality positive rate) overall and per group. Check for top-weighted tokens correlating with ethnicity. Healthy: similar token coverage and feature density across groups; class imbalance handled via reweighting/resampling. Unhealthy: certain ethnicity groups have systematically shorter notes or different vocabulary distributions (which could be a confounder), or severe class imbalance (typically ~10% mortality) untreated leads to majority-class collapse.
possible_sources: Boag et al., 'What's in a Note? Unpacking Predictive Value in Clinical Note Representations' (AMIA 2018)
Johnson et al., 'MIMIC-III, a freely accessible critical care database' (Sci Data 2016)
Chawla et al., 'SMOTE: Synthetic Minority Over-sampling Technique' (JAIR 2002)

probe_designs.content: Apply controlled perturbations: random TF-IDF feature dropout (e.g., zero out 5/10/20% of nonzero features), token swap with synonyms, or Gaussian noise on features. Measure stability of predicted mortality probability and AUROC. Healthy: small perturbations cause small changes in predicted probability (<0.05 mean shift) and AUROC degrades gracefully. Unhealthy: tiny perturbations flip many predictions, suggesting overreliance on a few sparse tokens (brittle keyword shortcuts).
possible_sources: Goodfellow et al., 'Explaining and Harnessing Adversarial Examples' (ICLR 2015)
Araujo et al., 'Stability of clinical NLP models under realistic perturbations' (CHIL 2021)
Wang & Bansal, 'Robust Machine Comprehension via Adversarial Training' (NAACL 2018)

probe_designs.probe_name: Top-feature attribution and clinical face validity
probe_designs.content: Extract top-k positive and negative TF-IDF tokens via model coefficients (linear) or SHAP/Integrated Gradients (nonlinear). Compare top tokens for predicting death vs. survival. Healthy: clinically meaningful tokens (e.g., 'cardiac arrest', 'sepsis', 'comfort care', 'pressors') dominate; few demographic or proxy tokens appear. Unhealthy: top features include ethnicity-correlated proxies (insurance type, zip, language indicators) or junk artifacts (timestamps, formatting), suggesting shortcut learning.
possible_sources: Lundberg & Lee, 'A Unified Approach to Interpreting Model Predictions' (NeurIPS 2017)
Sundararajan et al., 'Axiomatic Attribution for Deep Networks' (ICML 2017)
Caruana et al., 'Intelligible Models for HealthCare: Predicting Pneumonia Risk' (KDD 2015)

probe_designs.probe_name: Worst-group and intersectional performance via DRO lens
probe_designs.content: Beyond average per-group metrics, compute worst-group AUROC and AUPRC, and examine intersectional slices (ethnicity × age bucket, ethnicity × sex if available, ethnicity × note length). Track the gap between average and worst-group performance. Healthy: worst-group AUROC within ~0.03 of overall AUROC. Unhealthy: large worst-group degradation indicates the loss optimization favors majority groups; consider Group DRO or reweighting.
possible_sources: Sagawa et al., 'Distributionally Robust Neural Networks for Group Shifts' (ICLR 2020)
Koh et al., 'WILDS: A Benchmark of in-the-Wild Distribution Shifts' (ICML 2021)
Seyyed-Kalantari et al., 'Underdiagnosis bias of artificial intelligence algorithms applied to chest radiographs in under-served patient populations' (Nat Med 2021)

probe_designs.content: Compute precision-recall curves, decision curve analysis (net benefit), and identify operating thresholds that achieve clinically relevant sensitivity (e.g., 90%) per group. Compare positive predictive value, false alarm rate, and number-needed-to-alert across thresholds. Healthy: a single threshold yields acceptable sensitivity and PPV across all ethnicity groups. Unhealthy: a fixed threshold yields drastically different sensitivities per group (e.g., 0.9 for one, 0.6 for another), revealing operational unfairness even if AUROC is balanced.
possible_sources: Vickers & Elkin, 'Decision Curve Analysis: A Novel Method for Evaluating Prediction Models' (Med Decis Making 2006)
Saito & Rehmsmeier, 'The Precision-Recall Plot Is More Informative than the ROC Plot' (PLOS ONE 2015)
Pfohl et al., 'A comparison of approaches to improve worst-case predictive model performance over patient subpopulations' (Sci Rep 2022)

probe_designs.probe_name: Model capacity vs. sparse-input efficiency check
probe_designs.content: Given 10000-dim sparse TF-IDF input, compare a strong linear baseline (L2 logistic regression / linear SVM) against the deep model on the same split. Measure parameter count, training time, inference latency, and AUROC delta. Healthy: deep model meaningfully beats the linear baseline (>0.02 AUROC) and per-group disparities are not worse than the baseline. Unhealthy: deep model matches or underperforms the linear baseline while introducing higher disparity — suggesting wasted capacity, overfitting on sparse features, and unjustified complexity.
possible_sources: Joachims, 'Text Categorization with Support Vector Machines' (ECML 1998)
Rajkomar et al., 'Scalable and accurate deep learning with electronic health records' (npj Digital Medicine 2018)
Christodoulou et al., 'A systematic review shows no performance benefit of machine learning over logistic regression for clinical prediction' (J Clin Epidemiol 2019)
